title: What to Expect During Your Home Inspection
body:
Buying your first home in the Pioneer Valley usually means buying a home with some history. Much of the housing stock in Franklin, Hampshire, and Hampden Counties predates 1950, and even newer homes here carry quirks that a casual walkthrough will not reveal. A home inspection is how you find out what you are actually buying before you are committed to it.

This guide walks through the process from scheduling to report, so you know what to expect at every step. It reflects how Ken Arnold runs inspections across Western Massachusetts, and most of it applies no matter who inspects your home.

What happens during a home inspection?

A home inspection is a visual examination of every accessible system in the house, performed after your offer is accepted and before your inspection contingency expires. The inspector walks the property from the site grading to the roof, documents the condition of each system, and delivers a written report of the findings.

Ken performs every inspection personally and works to or above the ASHI Standards of Practice. He holds Massachusetts Home Inspector License #587 and has been licensed since 2004, with a construction and remodeling background before that. The same person who answers your scheduling call walks the property, signs the report, and answers your questions afterward.

How long does a home inspection take?

Plan on 2.5 to 3 hours on site for a standard home. Older or larger properties can run up to 4 hours, and in this region that matters. An 1890s farmhouse in Whately or a 1920s colonial in Northampton has more to look at than a 2005 build in Westfield: original framing, layered roofing, updated and not-quite-updated wiring, and a basement that has seen 100 years of water seasons.

Most inspections in the area book within 3 to 7 days, and time-sensitive bookings before a closing deadline can usually be accommodated. If you are under contract, schedule early in your contingency window so there is time to read the report and ask questions.

Should you attend the inspection?

Yes, if you can. Clients are encouraged to attend the full inspection. Walking the house with the inspector turns a 50-page report into something you understand, because you saw the furnace data plate, the panel interior, and the slope of the grade yourself.

If you cannot attend, the report is built to stand on its own, and follow-up calls and emails are included after delivery at no extra charge.

What does the inspection cover?

A full buyer inspection covers all accessible systems:

  • Structure and foundation
  • Roofing, flashing, gutters, and chimneys
  • Exterior siding, trim, windows, and doors
  • Electrical service, panel, and visible wiring
  • Plumbing supply, drains, and water heater
  • Heating and cooling systems
  • Insulation and ventilation
  • Interior floors, walls, ceilings, and stairs
  • Attached garage
  • Site grading and drainage

In Pioneer Valley homes, certain items get extra attention because they show up again and again: fieldstone foundations in older Deerfield homes, knob-and-tube wiring remnants in pre-1920s houses throughout the region, Federal Pacific electrical panels still common in Chicopee, and drainage issues on the steep hillside lots in Northampton's Florence neighborhood. Recognizing these patterns is the benefit of inspecting the same housing stock for over two decades. You can read the full scope on the buyer home inspection page.

What will the report look like?

You receive a photo-documented digital report, typically 40 to 80 pages, with findings organized by safety priority: safety hazards first, then major defects, then maintenance items. That order matters. A first-time buyer reading a long report needs to know immediately which three items deserve attention before closing and which twenty are normal upkeep for a home of that age.

Every finding includes photos and an explanation of why it matters. Because Ken built and remodeled homes before inspecting them, the report can tell you not just that something failed but why it failed and what fixing it generally involves. One Northampton couple used their report's documentation of foundation drainage issues to negotiate a $9,000 repair credit.

How much does an inspection cost?

Pricing is flat-fee and published, with no surprises and no upsells. A single-family inspection runs $600 to $750 depending on square footage. Specialty tests such as radon, well water, or termite can be bundled into the same visit at a lower rate than scheduling them separately. Current rates are on the pricing page.

Which add-on tests do first-time buyers actually need?

It depends on the property, not on a sales script. Two guidelines for this region:

  • Radon testing is worth considering for almost any home here. The Pioneer Valley sits on granite and pegmatite geology that produces elevated radon levels, and Western Massachusetts averages higher concentrations than much of the state. A 48-hour continuous monitor test answers the question for $125 when bundled with the inspection. Details are on the radon testing page.
  • Well water testing and a septic evaluation apply if the property is rural with private systems, which is common in Franklin County and the hilltowns. Homes on municipal water and sewer can skip both.

If a test does not fit your property, the honest answer is that you do not need it. Ken is independent, takes no referral fees, and has no incentive to add tests that do not apply.

Frequently asked questions

Will the inspection pass or fail the house?

No. A home inspection is not a pass/fail test and not a code inspection. It documents condition so you can decide how to proceed: move forward, negotiate repairs or credits, or walk away with your deposit intact under your contingency.

Should I expect the report to find problems?

Yes. Nearly every home in this region, at any price point, produces findings. The useful question is not whether issues exist but which ones are safety items, which are expensive, and which are routine maintenance for a home of that age. The report's safety-priority organization answers exactly that.

What if I have questions a week after closing?

Call or email. Follow-up support is included after the report is delivered, at no extra charge. First-time buyers often have a second round of questions once contractors start quoting repairs, and that conversation is part of the service.

Do I need to do anything to prepare?

No preparation is required on your end. Bring your questions and wear shoes you do not mind getting dusty if you plan to follow along into the basement.

Schedule your inspection

If you are under contract on a home anywhere in the Pioneer Valley, schedule your inspection online or call (413) 522-8004. Ken personally performs every inspection, and you are welcome to walk the property with him.
excerpt: What actually happens during a Massachusetts home inspection — from scheduling to the written report — and how long it takes, step by step.

hero.alt: Licensed home inspector examining the eaves and roofline of a white New England colonial home during a buyer's home inspection

seo.title: What to Expect: Home Inspection
seo.description: A step-by-step guide to what happens during a Massachusetts home inspection — scheduling, the walkthrough, timing, and the written report. From a 22-year MA inspector.
